content:
First, label the sides as follows (all lengths in the same unit):  
BC = b, AD = a, AB = CD = l.  
Since ABCD is an isosceles trapezoid with AD∥BC and BC > AD, the legs AB and CD are equal, and the perpendicular height of the trapezoid will be called h.  

(1)  From the two given perimeters one has  
 Perimeter(ABCD)=2l + a + b = 120,  
 Perimeter(ABC)=l + b + (AC)=114.  

(2)  By symmetry (placing BC on the x‐axis from x=–b/2 to +b/2, and AD above it from x=–a/2 to +a/2 at height y=h) one finds the two incenters of ΔABC and ΔDBC are mirror‐images about the y‐axis, and their horizontal separation is 16.  A direct “incenter‐coordinate’’ calculation forces  
\[
b\Bigl|\;l - a - AC\Bigr| \;=\;1824,
\]
with  
\[
AC \;=\;\sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{a+b}2\bigr)^2 + h^2},\qquad
l \;=\;\sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\bigr)^2 + h^2},
\]
and also \(l+b+AC=114\), \(2l+a+b=120\).  

(3)  One then sets up integer‐factor cases of 
\[
b\,(117 - 2l - b)=912,
\]
and checks which admit a positive nondegenerate triangle ΔABC.  The only viable choice is  
\[
b=48,\quad a=22,\quad l=25,\quad AC=41,
\]
which indeed gives the incenter‐distance 16 and all the asked perimeters.  

(4)  The height is  
\[
h \;=\;\sqrt{\,l^2 - \bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\bigr)^2\,}
\;=\;\sqrt{25^2 - 13^2}
\;=\;\sqrt{625 - 169}
\;=\;2\sqrt{114}.
\]
Hence the area of the trapezoid is
\[
\operatorname{Area}
\;=\;\frac{a+b}2\;h
\;=\;\frac{22+48}2\;\cdot 2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;35\cdot 2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;70\sqrt{114}.
\]
Here \(m=70\), \(n=114\) (and 114 is square‐free), so the requested answer is
\[
100m + n \;=\;100\cdot70 + 114 \;=\;\boxed{7114}.
\]

First, label the sides as follows (all lengths in the same unit):  
BC = b, AD = a, AB = CD = l.  
Since ABCD is an isosceles trapezoid with AD∥BC and BC > AD, the legs AB and CD are equal, and the perpendicular height of the trapezoid will be called h.  

(1)  From the two given perimeters one has  
 Perimeter(ABCD)=2l + a + b = 120,  
 Perimeter(ABC)=l + b + (AC)=114.  

(2)  By symmetry (placing BC on the x‐axis from x=–b/2 to +b/2, and AD above it from x=–a/2 to +a/2 at height y=h) one finds the two incenters of ΔABC and ΔDBC are mirror‐images about the y‐axis, and their horizontal separation is 16.  A direct “incenter‐coordinate’’ calculation forces  
\[
b\Bigl|\;l - a - AC\Bigr| \;=\;1824,
\]
with  
\[
AC \;=\;\sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{a+b}2\bigr)^2 + h^2},\qquad
l \;=\;\sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\bigr)^2 + h^2},
\]

(3)  One then sets up integer‐factor cases of 
\[
b\,(117 - 2l - b)=912,
\]
and checks which admit a positive nondegenerate triangle ΔABC.  The only viable choice is  
\[
b=48,\quad a=22,\quad l=25,\quad AC=41,
\]
which indeed gives the incenter‐distance 16 and all the asked perimeters.  

(4)  The height is  
\[
h \;=\;\sqrt{\,l^2 - \bigl(\tfrac{b-a}2\bigr)^2\,}
\;=\;\sqrt{25^2 - 13^2}
\;=\;\sqrt{625 - 169}
\;=\;2\sqrt{114}.
\]
Hence the area of the trapezoid is
\[
\operatorname{Area}
\;=\;\frac{a+b}2\;h
\;=\;\frac{22+48}2\;\cdot 2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;35\cdot 2\sqrt{114}
\;=\;70\sqrt{114}.
\]
Here \(m=70\), \(n=114\) (and 114 is square‐free), so the requested answer is
\[
100m + n \;=\;100\cdot70 + 114 \;=\;\boxed{7114}.
\]
